text: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutations in non-small cell lung cancer are well-established markers of sensitivity to EGFR-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy, with strong, mature evidence supporting first-line use.
ALK gene rearrangements in lung cancer identify a subset of tumors highly sensitive to ALK-targeted inhibitors, generally preferred over cytotoxic chemotherapy when detected.
HER2 gene amplification or protein overexpression in breast cancer supports the use of HER2-targeted antibody or small-molecule inhibitor therapy, with well-established outcome benefit.
Pathogenic BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations are associated with sensitivity to PARP-inhibitor class therapy across several tumor types, reflecting a defect in homologous recombination DNA repair.

KRAS G12C mutations were historically considered undruggable, but investigational KRAS G12C-directed inhibitors are an emerging option, generally accessed through clinical trial enrollment or newer targeted approvals depending on tumor type and prior therapy.

Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or mismatch repair deficient (dMMR) tumor status is associated with strong responsiveness to checkpoint immunotherapy, largely independent of tumor site of origin.
High PD-L1 expression is associated with a greater likelihood of benefit from checkpoint immunotherapy, though the association is generally less strong and less universal than MSI-H/dMMR status.

MSI-H or dMMR status co-occurring with a reported low tumor mutational burden is an atypical combination that can reflect assay or sampling issues, and generally warrants confirmatory repeat or orthogonal testing before finalizing a therapy decision on either marker alone.

Estrogen and progesterone receptor-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer generally responds well to hormone (endocrine) therapy, often allowing continuation of well-tolerated regimens when disease is stable.
Triple-negative breast cancer lacks the three most common targetable receptors, so treatment decisions lean more heavily on chemotherapy and, when PD-L1 positive, checkpoint immunotherapy.

A patient's performance status (commonly graded on the ECOG scale) is a key factor in tolerability of intensive systemic therapy; poor performance status generally favors lower-intensity approaches or a greater emphasis on supportive and palliative care.

Shared decision-making that incorporates a patient's stated goals and preferences -- for example, prioritizing quality of life or minimizing hospital visits -- is considered good practice alongside purely biological treatment matching.
New lesions or unambiguous growth of existing lesions on follow-up imaging generally indicate disease progression and prompt reconsideration of the current therapy line.
Stable disease or partial response on follow-up imaging generally supports continuing the current therapy line, absent new tolerability concerns.
